subject: pulmonology
title: Asthma
preambleMarkdown:
# Asthma



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Asthma is a clinical condition or syndrome in pulmonology.
- This article focuses on airway, ventilation, oxygenation, imaging, pulmonary function, respiratory failure, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is pulmonology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Asthma to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Asthma is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is pulmonology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Asthma depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In pulmonology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Asthma.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Asthma disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Asthma should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Asthma first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Asthma, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Asthma, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused pulmonology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Asthma presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Asthma with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Asthma is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Asthma should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Asthma is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses pulmonology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Asthma, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Asthma may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Asthma patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Asthma, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Asthma in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Asthma, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Asthma does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Asthma may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Asthma, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Asthma as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Asthma draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Asthma

_Source lines: 6343-6620._

```text
Asthma
A. General Characteristics
1. Characteristically defined by the following triad:

a. Airway inflammation
b. Airway hyperresponsiveness
c. Reversible airflow obstruction
2. Asthma can begin at any age
3. Extrinsic versus intrinsic asthma
a. Extrinsic asthma (most cases)
- Patients are atopic, that is, produce immunoglobulin E (IgE)
to environmental antigens. May be associated with eczema
and hay fever.

- Patients become asthmatic at a young age.

d. Intrinsic asthma-not related to atopy or environmental triggers
4. Triggers include aeroallergens (pollens, house dust, molds,
cockroaches, animals), irritants (tobacco smoke, cannabis,
woodburning, fumes), cold air, exercise, viral infections, and
medications (beta-blockers, aspirin, certain eye drops)
CLINICAL PEARL 2-2
"All That Wheezes Is Not Asthma"
The most common cause of wheezing is asthma. However, any condition that
mimics large airway bronchospasm can cause wheezing.

- CHF-due to edema of airways and congestion of bronchial mucosa
- COPD-inflamed airways may be narrowed, or bronchospasm may be
present
- Cardiomyopathies, pericardial diseases can lead to edema around the
bronchi
- Lung cancer-due to obstruction of airways (central tumor or mediastinal
invasion)
Quick HIT
Signs of acute severe asthma attacks:
- Tachypnea, diaphoresis, wheezing, speaking in incomplete
sentences, and use of accessory muscles of respiration.

- Paradoxic movement of the abdomen and diaphragm on
inspiration is sign of impending respiratory failure.

B. Clinical Features
1. Characterized by intermittent symptoms that include SOB,
wheezing, chest tightness, and cough. Symptoms have variable

severity and may not be present simultaneously. Usually occur
within 30 minutes of exposure to triggers.

2. Symptoms are typically worse at night.

3. Wheezing (commonly during expiration, but can occur during
inspiration) is the most common finding on physical examination
(see Clinical Pearl 2-2).

C. Diagnosis
1. Pulmonary function tests (PFTs) are required for diagnosis. They
show an obstructive pattern: decrease in expiratory flow rates,
decreased FEV 1 , and decreased FEV 1/FVC ratio (<0.70). Note that
PFTs may be normal if asymptomatic at the time. These patients
may require repeat testing during symptoms or bronchoprovocation
testing.

2. Spirometry before and after bronchodilators can confirm diagnosis
by proving reversible airway obstruction. If inhalation of a
bronchodilator (beta 2-agonist) results in an increase in FEV 1 or FVC by
at least 12%, airflow obstruction is considered reversible.

3. Peak flow (peak expiratory flow rate)-useful measure of airflow
obstruction, but can be limited by patient effort and technique.

Patients should self-monitor their peak flow:
a. Mild persistent asthma: Periodic monitoring is suficient.

Increase the dose of inhaled steroid, if the peak flow decreases.

b. Moderate persistent asthma: Daily monitoring is required.

Increase the dose of inhaled steroid, if the peak flow decreases.

c. Severe persistent asthma: Daily monitoring is required. Initiate
prednisone, if the peak flow decreases.

4. Bronchoprovocation test
a. Useful when asthma is suspected but PFTs are nondiagnostic.

b. Measures ease with which airways narrow in response to stimuli
(hyperresponsiveness).

c. Measures lung function before and after inhalation of increasing
doses of methacholine (muscarinic agonist); hyperresponsive
airways develop obstruction at lower doses.

## 5. CXR

a. Normal in mild to moderate cases; severe asthma may show
hyperinflation.

b. Only necessary in severe asthma to exclude other conditions
(e.g., pneumonia, pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum, foreign
body).

## 6. ABGs

a. ABGs should be considered if the patient is in significant
respiratory distress. Hypocarbia is common. Hypoxemia may
be present.

b. If the PaCO 2 is normal or increased, respiratory failure may
ensue.

- Remember that patients with an asthma attack have an
increased RR, which should cause the PaCO 2 to decrease.

Increasing PaCO 2 is a sign of respiratory muscle fatigue or
severe airway obstruction.

- The patient should be hospitalized and mechanical
ventilation considered.

Quick HIT
PFTs in asthma:

## 1. Decreased FEV1, decreased FVC, decreased FEV 1 /FVC ratio

2. Increase in FEV 1 >12% with albuterol
3. Decrease in FEV 1 >20% with methacholine or histamine
Quick HIT
Although asthma can be diagnosed with PFTs and spirometry, in an
acute setting (ED) when patient is SOB, history and observed
response to antiasthma medication is quickest method of diagnosis.

TABLE 2-3 Chronic Treatment of Asthma
Severity 	Initial Therapy
Mild intermittent (symptoms two or
fewer times per week)
SABA as needed
Low-dose ICS as needed
Mild persistent (symptoms two or
more times per week but not every
day)
Low-dose ICS daily
Low-dose ICS + LABA combination
inhaler as needed
Moderate persistent (daily
symptoms; frequent exacerbations)
Low-dose ICS + LABA combination
inhaler daily
Severe persistent (continual
symptoms, frequent exacerbations,
limited physical activity)
Medium-dose ICS + LABA combination
inhaler daily
Consider add-on therapies like tiotropium,
biologics like omalizumab (anti-IgE),
systemic corticosteroids, others
Note: All patients should have intermittent short-acting inhaled beta 2 -agonists as needed plus
long-term control medications based on the severity of their asthma.

ICS, inhaled corticosteroid; SABA, short-acting beta-agonist; LABA, long-acting beta-agonist.

From The National Asthma Education and Prevention Program, Expert Panel Report III:
2007.

Quick HIT
During asthma exacerbations, the patient hyperventilates, leading to
low PaCO2 levels. If the patient is no longer hyperventilating (CO 2
level is normal or high), this could be a sign that the patient is
decompensating (due to fatigue) and that intubation may be
required.

Quick HIT
Avoid nonselective beta-blockers in asthmatics!

D. Treatment
1. Available modalities (see Table 2-3)
a. Inhaled beta 2-agonists
- Short-acting beta 2-agonists (SABA, e.g., albuterol) are used for
acute attacks (rescue). Onset is 2 to 5 minutes, duration is 4
to 6 hours.

- Long-acting beta 2-agonists (LABA, e.g., salmeterol) are often
used in combination with inhaled corticosteroids for severe
or persistent symptoms.

d. Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) for moderate to severe asthma
- Preferred over oral steroids due to fewer systemic side
effects. (Oral steroids are reserved for severe, persistent
asthma.)

- If used on a regular basis, airway hyperresponsiveness
decreases, and the frequency of asthma exacerbations
decreases.

g. Montelukast-leukotriene modifiers-limited evidence but may
be useful for prophylaxis of mild exercise-induced asthma and
for control of moderate persistent disease. They may allow
reductions in steroid and bronchodilator requirements
h. Theophylline
- Can be useful in addition to ICS for persistent symptoms.

2. Treatment of acute severe asthma exacerbation (hospital
admission)
a. Inhaled beta 2 -agonist (first-line therapy)
- Via nebulizer or MDI (see Clinical Pearl 2-3)
- Mainstays of emergency treatment-have an onset of action

of minutes
- Assess patient response to bronchodilators (clinically and
with peak flows)
e. Corticosteroids
- Traditionally given intravenously initially, but may also be
given orally if given in equivalent doses.

- Taper IV or oral corticosteroids, but only when clinical
improvement is seen.

- Initiate inhaled corticosteroids at the beginning of the
tapering schedule.

i. Third-line agent includes IV magnesium-not as effective as beta-
agonists; magnesium helps with bronchospasm but only used in
acute severe exacerbation that has not responded to above
medications (albuterol, steroids, oxygen).

j. Supplemental oxygen (keep oxygen saturation >90%).

k. Antibiotics, only if suspicion of bacterial pneumonia (as most
triggers are viral).

l. Intubation for patients in respiratory failure or impending
respiratory failure.

m. Guidelines for treatment are stepwise based on severity, which
depends on frequency (intermittent vs. persistent) and symptom
severity (mild, moderate, or severe). See Table 2-3.

CLINICAL PEARL 2-3
Metered-Dose Inhalers (MDIs) and Nebulizers
- An MDI with a spacer is just as effective as a nebulizer. A spacer is a
holding chamber that obviates the need to coordinate inhalation and
depression of the canister, and thus makes the use of an MDI easier. Its
use leads to a greater bronchodilator effect because more of the drug is
deposited in smaller airways and less accumulates in the oropharynx.

- A nebulizer is no more effective than an MDI, but patients may report
greater relief of symptoms simply because it is more comfortable (patient
does not have to coordinate breathing with medication administration). It
may be preferred by patients with very severe asthma unresponsive to
MDIs.

Quick HIT
- Side effects of inhaled corticosteroids are due to oropharyngeal
deposition and include sore throat, oral candidiasis (thrush),

and hoarseness.

- Using a spacer with MDIs and rinsing the mouth after use help
minimize these side effects.

Quick HIT
For acute asthma exacerbation, test to order:
1. Peak expiratory flow-decreased

## 2. ABG-increased A-a gradient

3. CXR-rule out pneumonia, pneumothorax
```

### Source Excerpt: Asthma

_Source lines: 6941-7177._

```text
II. ASTHMA
A. Discussion
1. Definition. Asthma is defined as the presence of intermittent symptoms from
airway hyperactivity and reversible airway obstruction due to multiple stimuli.

Airway obstruction is usually caused by smooth muscle contraction and airway
inflammation.

a. Extrinsic asthma is immunologically mediated and tends to develop in
childhood.

b. Intrinsic asthma has no identifiable cause and tends to worsen with age.

2. Incidence. Asthma affects approximately 5% of adults in the United States;
approximately 1.5 million patients seek care for asthma in the emergency
department (ED) each year.

B. Clinical features

## 1. Symptoms

a. Wheezing , dyspnea, and cough are most common. In cough-variant
asthma, cough, which is often worse at night, is the prominent feature.

b. Pleuritic chest pain is noted in some patients.

c. Accessory muscle use may be seen in patients having moderate to severe
attacks.

d. Altered mental status, severely labored breathing, or extreme fatigue are
signs of impending respiratory arrest.

2. Physical examination findings
a. Tachypnea and tachycardia are common.

b. Pulmonary examination often demonstrates wheezing , a prolonged expira-
tory phase, and decreased breath sounds.

C. Differential diagnoses include CHF, upper airway obstruction, foreign body aspi-
ration, pulmonary embolism, anaphylactic reactions, pneumonia, croup, bronchi-
olitis, COPD, and toxic inhalation.

D. Evaluation
1. Pulmonary function tests. The peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) can be used
to evaluate the degree of obstruction (Table 3-1).

2. Pulse oximetry can be used to evaluate the degree of hypoxia.

3. Chest radiographs should be ordered for patients with hypoxia, fever, a
focal lung examination, or symptoms not responsive to bronchodilator
therapy.

4. ABG. ABGs should not be ordered routinely but should be reserved for
patients with severe attacks or suspected respiratory failure. Because a pa-
tient having an asthma exacerbation is breathing quickly, his or her arterial
carbon dioxide tension (Pa CO 2 ) should be less than 40 mm Hg (respiratory
alkalosis).

E. Therapy
1. Oxygen is indicated for all asthma patients to keep the oxygen saturation
greater than 95%.

Quick 	HIT
In severe asthma, when
airflow is very low, wheezing
may be absent.

Quick 	HIT
A normal or elevated PaCO 2
in a patient with an asthma
exacerbation represents im-
pending or actual respiratory
failure.

Severity 	Signs and Symptoms 	Initial PEF Rate as % of Predicted
Mild 	Dyspnea with activity 	PEF "70% predicted
Moderate 	Dyspnea interferes with activity 	PEF 40%-69% predicted
Severe 	Dyspnea at rest 	PEF #40% predicted
Life-threatening 	Too dyspneic to speak 	PEF #25% predicted
PEF, peak expiratory flow.

Asthma Severity Assessment	TABLE 3-1
86 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Pulmonary Emergencies
2. Pharmacologic therapy
a. Standard (first-line) agents
i. Inhaled short-acting !2 agonists act as bronchial smooth muscle re-
laxants. Nebulized albuterol is administered every 15 to 20 minutes;
in patients with severe attacks, these agents may be administered
continuously.

ii. Corticosteroids suppress the inflammatory component of asthma
and are considered first-line agents for the treatment of patients with
asthma that do not respond rapidly to inhaled !2 agonists.

a) Oral prednisone (60 mg in adults, 2 mg/kg in children) may be
administered. Methylprednisolone (125 mg in adults, 2 mg/kg in
children) may be administered intravenously if the patient is unable
to tolerate oral prednisone secondary to vomiting or respiratory dis-
tress. Dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg) is a viable alternative and allows
for less frequent dosing than prednisone.

b) Patients who have been administered steroids in the ED who are
subsequently discharged to home should continue to take oral
steroids for 5 days (e.g., prednisone, 40 to 60 mg once daily for
adults or 1 mg/kg/day for children; dexamethasone one further dose
72 hours from the first dose) to reduce the rate of asthma relapse.

iii. Anticholinergic agents relax bronchial smooth muscle, particularly in
the more proximal airways. Although these agents are most useful in
the treatment of COPD, they may also be used for patients with asthma
exacerbations that do not rapidly respond to inhaled !2 agonists.

a) Metered-dose or nebulized ipratropium bromide can be adminis-
tered for up to three doses.

b) Ipratropium and albuterol solutions may be mixed together and
administered in the same handheld nebulizer.

b. Second-line agents
i. Magnesium sulfate (2 g intravenously over 30 minutes) should be con-
sidered in adults with severe asthma exacerbations not responsive to
initial therapy.

ii. Epinephrine (0.3 mg subcutaneously in adults or 0.01 mg/kg in chil-
dren, up to 0.3 mg) or terbutaline (0.25 mg subcutaneously in adults or
0.01 mg/kg/dose for children, maximum dose is 0.4 mg) can be consid-
ered for young patients with severe asthma not responsive to first-line
agents.

c. Other pharmacologic options
i. Inhaled general anesthetics are potent bronchodilators. Their use in
the treatment of asthma is reserved for patients with severe asthma
exacerbations in whom previous therapy has not resulted in adequate
oxygenation or ventilation.

ii. Ketamine is a dissociative anesthetic agent that also causes bronchodi-
lation and can be used for severe, refractory asthma.

a) An initial bolus of 1 to 2 mg/kg is administered intravenously
over 30 to 60 minutes, followed by an intravenous ketamine drip
(1 mg/kg/hour).

b) Ketamine is contraindicated in patients with ischemic heart disease,
increased intracranial pressure, severe hypertension, or preeclampsia.

iii. Heliox , a mixture of 80% helium and 20% oxygen, can be administered
via face mask or nebulizer to patients with severe, refractory asthma.

In nonintubated patients, heliox decreases the work of breathing by im-
proving laminar gas flow and airway resistance. In intubated patients,
heliox results in improved oxygenation and decreased peak airway
pressures.

3. Intubation and mechanical ventilation. Intubation is indicated for patients
with impending respiratory failure. Orotracheal intubation is preferred over na-
sotracheal intubation because orotracheal intubation allows for the placement

Patients who are steroid-
dependent or who have used
steroids recently should be
gradually tapered off the
steroids over 10 to 14 days
rather than discontinuing
steroids after 5 days.

Quick 	HIT
Epinephrine should not be
used during early pregnancy
or in patients suspected

P U L M O N A R Y E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	87

Pulmonary Emergencies
of a larger endotracheal tube, which decreases the work of breathing (by lower-
ing airway resistance) and facilitates the management of pulmonary secretions.

a. Rapid sequence intubation (see Chapter 1.III.B.3.c) should be considered
for asthmatic patients requiring intubation.

i. Ketamine (2 mg/kg intravenously), because of its bronchodilation
properties, is a good choice of induction agent.

ii. Benzodiazepines, rather than opiates, should be used for sedation in
intubated patients with asthma because opiates may induce histamine
release from mast cells, exacerbating the bronchospasm.

iii. Pancuronium or vecuronium may be administered to induce muscle
paralysis if ventilation is difficult following intubation.

b. Complications
i. Barotrauma is a common complication of mechanical ventilation in pa-
tients with asthma. To avoid this complication, the tidal volume should
be 6 to 8 mL/kg, and the ventilator should be adjusted to increase the
time allowed for expiration to minimize peak airway pressures.

ii. Hypotension that develops rapidly after intubation and mechanical ven-
tilation usually results from the auto-positive end-expiratory pressure
(auto-PEEP) phenomenon or a tension pneumothorax.

a) Auto-PEEP phenomenon. Air-trapping in the lungs can lead to in-
creased intrathoracic pressure, which may result in decreased venous
return and a resultant decrease in cardiac output. If air-trapping is
suspected as the cause of hypotension, the respiratory rate on the
ventilator should be decreased to allow adequate time for exhalation.

b) Pneumothorax. A tension pneumothorax may develop as a result
of air-trapping and increased airway pressures, leading to kinking
of the mediastinal vessels, a decrease in venous return to the heart,
and decreased cardiac output. If tension pneumothorax is suspected,
immediate needle decompression of the affected side should be
undertaken, followed by chest tube placement.

F. 	Disposition
1. Admission. In a patient with asthma, evidence of pneumonia is generally an
indication for hospital admission. Patients with persistent symptoms despite
treatment and continued PEFR of # 40% of predicted value should be admitted
to the hospital.

## 2. Discharge

a. Patients may be discharged to home if they have made significant clinical
improvement, have a PEFR greater than 70% of the predicted value
(or equal to their baseline PEFR), have a normal respiratory rate, and are
able to walk without recurring symptoms.

b. Patients with a PEFR that is 40% to 60% of the predicted value may be dis-
charged if they are otherwise stable, compliant, and have a good follow-up
system.
```
